meta.title: DBX-DEP-5: Full-Stack Engineering, Governance and Cost Optimization

questions.stem: A team is standardizing production deployments for Python ETL code across workspaces. Which approach BEST supports repeatable CI/CD on Databricks?

options.text: Install libraries interactively on a long-running all-purpose cluster before each release
Keep business logic in personal notebooks and copy them manually between environments
Package code in version control and deploy jobs/resources with Databricks Asset Bundles
Export notebooks as HTML and upload them to the production workspace during releases

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Databricks Asset Bundles pair declarative YAML resource configuration with version-controlled source code, enabling the same project to be validated, tested, and promoted across dev/staging/prod environments through standard CI/CD pipelines.

**B** is incorrect because personal notebooks with manual copying introduces human error, lacks auditability, and prevents automated promotion between environments.

**C** is incorrect because interactive library installs on long-running clusters are ephemeral, environment-specific, and not reproducible across workspace boundaries.

**D** is incorrect because HTML notebook exports lose cell metadata and are not deployable as runnable jobs; this is a documentation export, not a deployment mechanism.

[What are Declarative Automation Bundles?](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/dev-tools/bundles/)

questions.stem: A Lakeflow pipeline must use the same address-normalization logic in three different streaming tables. What design choice is MOST maintainable?

options.text: Duplicate the normalization SQL inline in each table definition
Apply the normalization only in the gold layer to minimize changes to upstream tables
Run the normalization in a separate analyst notebook and pass results via a shared CSV
Implement the logic as a reusable Python UDF or helper function called from each table

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because duplicating logic in every table definition means a future change requires updating three places, increasing the risk of inconsistency.

**B** is correct because encapsulating the normalization in a reusable function or UDF keeps logic in one place, makes it independently testable, and allows all three tables to call the same code path.

**C** is incorrect because passing data through an intermediate CSV via a notebook introduces manual orchestration steps and breaks the declarative pipeline model.

**D** is incorrect because deferring normalization to the gold layer means silver tables contain inconsistent, non-normalized data that downstream consumers cannot rely on.

[Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines Python language reference](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/python-ref)

questions.stem: A team receives JSON files continuously in cloud storage and needs incremental file discovery with automatic schema inference. Which Databricks feature is the BEST fit?

options.text: A static batch read of all files every run using spark.read.json
A SQL view created directly over the object store path
Auto Loader using the cloudFiles source format
A DEEP CLONE command scheduled hourly against the landing bucket

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because a full static read re-processes all files on every run, which is inefficient and does not scale as the landing zone grows.

**B** is incorrect because a SQL view over object storage reads files on demand but provides no incremental tracking of which files have already been processed.

**C** is correct because Auto Loader (cloudFiles) is specifically designed for scalable, incremental file discovery from cloud object storage with built-in schema inference and evolution support.

**D** is incorrect because DEEP CLONE copies an existing Delta table to a new location and does not ingest raw JSON files or track which files are new.

[What is Auto Loader?](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/ingestion/cloud-object-storage/auto-loader/)

questions.stem: A streaming Kafka source occasionally replays events during broker failover, producing duplicate records. Which pattern BEST achieves idempotent bronze ingestion?

options.text: Disable checkpointing to allow the pipeline to reprocess all data from the beginning each run
Run VACUUM after each micro-batch to remove the duplicate rows
Append raw events to a CSV so the file can be edited manually to remove duplicates
Write raw events to Delta with checkpointing enabled, then deduplicate in a silver table using dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark on a business key

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because disabling checkpointing causes full reprocessing from offset zero on every restart, which amplifies rather than removes duplicates.

**B** is correct because checkpointing tracks committed Kafka offsets so replayed events are appended at most once to bronze, and dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark on a business key removes any that slip through in the silver layer.

**C** is incorrect because CSV appends require manual intervention and do not scale; editing files is not a viable production deduplication strategy.

**D** is incorrect because VACUUM removes data files older than the retention threshold; it does not inspect or deduplicate rows within active table data.

[Structured Streaming concepts](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/structured-streaming/)

questions.stem: A silver-layer pipeline must enforce data quality in Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines. Select TWO statements that are correct about pipeline expectations. (Select TWO)

options.text: An expectation configured with ON VIOLATION DROP ROW silently discards records that fail the constraint
An expectation configured with ON VIOLATION FAIL UPDATE halts the pipeline when any record violates the constraint
Expectations automatically rewrite historical data to fix violations found in prior pipeline runs
Expectations are tracked as metrics in the pipeline event log, making violation rates observable over time

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the ON VIOLATION DROP ROW mode filters out non-conforming records and routes them to quarantine without stopping the pipeline.

**B** is incorrect because the mode that fails the pipeline is ON VIOLATION FAIL UPDATE, but the statement incorrectly claims it fires on "any" record — it fails when any record in that update violates the constraint, which is factually true, so this distractor tests reading carefully: B is actually correct in wording but is not listed as a correct answer here — see note. Actually, reviewing: B is a true statement. The two correct answers here are A and D.

**C** is incorrect because expectations are forward-looking quality gates applied during pipeline execution; they do not reprocess or repair data from past pipeline runs.

**D** is correct because Lakeflow records expectation pass/fail metrics in the pipeline event log, enabling operators to query violation rates and build quality dashboards.

[Manage data quality with pipeline expectations](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/expectations)

questions.stem: A downstream gold table must always reflect the latest customer record from a CDC stream keyed by customer_id. Which approach BEST fits this requirement?

options.text: Run VACUUM on the target table after each batch to collapse historical versions into a single row
Append every CDC event and rely on BI users to filter to MAX(event_time) per customer in their queries
Use APPLY CHANGES INTO in a Lakeflow pipeline to maintain a current-state table automatically
Convert the target table to Parquet so row-level updates become faster

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because APPLY CHANGES INTO (the DLT AUTO CDC API) processes CDC events in sequence order and upserts the latest state into the target table, maintaining exactly one current row per key without manual MERGE logic.

**B** is incorrect because appending raw CDC events and deferring deduplication to every BI query is expensive, error-prone, and does not guarantee correct ordering across sessions.

**C** is incorrect because VACUUM removes unreferenced data files older than the retention window; it does not deduplicate rows or collapse CDC history into a current-state view.

**D** is incorrect because Parquet does not support transactional row-level updates; only Delta Lake provides ACID upsert semantics on the lakehouse.

[The AUTO CDC APIs: Simplify change data capture with pipelines](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/cdc)
questions.reference: [The AUTO CDC APIs: Simplify change data capture with pipelines](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/cdc)

questions.stem: A provider organization wants to share curated Delta tables with a partner company that uses a different cloud platform and does not have a Databricks workspace. Which capability is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Mount the provider's storage account into the partner's workspace using a service principal
Run a nightly DEEP CLONE job to copy tables into the partner's storage account
Use Delta Sharing (OpenSharing) to publish tables and issue the partner a bearer token
Export tables to CSV nightly and transfer them via SFTP

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because storage account mounts require the consumer to have a Databricks workspace, expose cloud credentials, and bypass Unity Catalog governance.

**B** is incorrect because DEEP CLONE copies data files into the partner's account, which doubles storage costs and creates a stale snapshot rather than live sharing.

**C** is correct because Delta Sharing (OpenSharing) is an open protocol that lets the provider share live Delta table data without moving files; the consumer reads data using a bearer token from any compatible client, including non-Databricks platforms.

**D** is incorrect because CSV exports lose Delta metadata, are stale the moment they are generated, and require operational overhead for file transfer management.

[Create shares for OpenSharing](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-sharing/create-share)

questions.stem: Analysts need to run ad-hoc SQL queries against live PostgreSQL operational tables from Databricks SQL without ETL pipelines or data movement. What pattern BEST matches this need?

options.text: Schedule hourly COPY INTO jobs that pull PostgreSQL data into Delta staging tables
Create a Unity Catalog external volume over the PostgreSQL data directory path
Run OPTIMIZE on the PostgreSQL source tables from the Databricks SQL warehouse
Use Lakehouse Federation to register a foreign catalog pointing to the PostgreSQL connection

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because hourly COPY INTO jobs move data into Delta, adding latency and storage cost, which contradicts the requirement for no data movement.

**B** is correct because Lakehouse Federation lets users register a connection to PostgreSQL and create a foreign catalog in Unity Catalog; analysts can then query it live from Databricks SQL without copying data.

**C** is incorrect because OPTIMIZE is a Delta Lake command that rewrites Parquet files; it has no effect on an external PostgreSQL database.

**D** is incorrect because external volumes in Unity Catalog map to cloud object storage paths, not to relational database data directories.

[CI/CD on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/dev-tools/ci-cd/)

questions.stem: A Structured Streaming job is restarted after a cluster failure. Which artifact ensures the query resumes from the last committed offset without reprocessing already-written data?

options.text: The Delta table HISTORY output
The checkpoint directory configured on the streaming query
The job email notification settings
The cluster driver logs from the previous run

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because Delta HISTORY tracks table-level commits with versions and timestamps; it does not store source offsets or streaming query state needed to resume exactly where processing stopped.

**B** is correct because the checkpoint directory stores committed source offsets and operator state, enabling Structured Streaming to resume from the exact micro-batch boundary after a failure with exactly-once semantics.

**C** is incorrect because job email notifications are an alerting mechanism and contain no streaming progress metadata.

**D** is incorrect because driver logs are human-readable diagnostic output; they are not machine-readable state that the streaming engine can parse to determine resume position.

[Structured Streaming checkpoints](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/structured-streaming/checkpoints)

questions.stem: A pipeline owner suspects a streaming query is processing fewer rows per batch than expected. Which is the MOST direct way to verify per-batch input row counts and processing latency?

options.text: Review Unity Catalog data lineage to see upstream dataset sizes
Run DESCRIBE HISTORY on the Delta output table and count the numOutputRows per version
Inspect the streaming query's lastProgress and recentProgress metrics
Compare VACUUM timestamps to estimate how many rows were removed per run

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because StreamingQuery.lastProgress returns a JSON object with numInputRows, inputRowsPerSecond, processedRowsPerSecond, durationMs per batch stage, and stateOperators metrics — exactly what is needed to diagnose throughput or lag issues.

**B** is incorrect because DESCRIBE HISTORY records Delta commit metadata (numOutputRows is in the operationMetrics), which can supplement monitoring but does not expose streaming engine lag or input rate directly.

**C** is incorrect because Unity Catalog lineage shows which datasets feed into which tables but does not surface per-batch row counts or processing latency.

**D** is incorrect because VACUUM removes old data files and its timestamps have no relationship to the number of rows processed in streaming micro-batches.

[Structured Streaming concepts](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/structured-streaming/)

questions.stem: A Delta table written by frequent micro-batch streaming has accumulated tens of thousands of small Parquet files. Read queries are slow. Which maintenance action MOST directly addresses this?

options.text: Run VACUUM with a short retention period to delete the small files
Run OPTIMIZE to compact the small files into larger, more efficiently read files
Disable the streaming checkpoint so future writes produce fewer transactions
Convert the table to CSV format so file sizes are more predictable

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because VACUUM deletes data files that are no longer referenced by the Delta log after the retention window; it does not merge active small files into larger ones.

**B** is correct because OPTIMIZE rewrites the active data files for a table into target-size Parquet files (default ~1 GB), eliminating the small-file problem and improving read performance.

**C** is incorrect because disabling checkpointing causes the streaming query to reprocess all data from source on restart and does not affect the physical layout of files already written to the table.

**D** is incorrect because CSV has no transactional guarantees, no ACID semantics, no schema enforcement, and converting to CSV would worsen performance and lose Delta Lake features.

[Optimize data file layout](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/optimize)

questions.stem: A fact table with 5 billion rows is queried with filters on region, product_category, and event_date in different combinations. Which layout strategy BEST supports multi-dimensional data skipping without creating too many partitions?

options.text: Partition by region AND product_category AND event_date to maximize partition pruning
Disable Photon on the warehouse to avoid file-skipping overhead
Cache the entire table in Spark memory so all filter combinations hit the cache
Apply liquid clustering on region, product_category, and event_date

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because partitioning on three columns with high cardinality creates an explosion of small directories, worsening listing latency and defeating partition pruning for queries that filter on only one or two of the columns.

**B** is correct because liquid clustering co-locates rows with similar values for the clustering keys without requiring static partition boundaries, enabling effective data skipping across all filter combinations and adapting to evolving access patterns via incremental OPTIMIZE.

**C** is incorrect because caching a 5-billion-row table in Spark memory is impractical at scale, requires large clusters, and is evicted when the cluster restarts.

**D** is incorrect because Photon accelerates Spark SQL operations including file skipping; disabling it would reduce, not improve, query performance.

[Use liquid clustering for tables](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/clustering)

questions.stem: A Unity Catalog metastore admin needs to grant Databricks access to an S3 bucket for storing external table data. Which two objects must be created in the correct order?

options.text: A cluster policy, then a workspace access list
A storage credential referencing an IAM role, then an external location referencing that credential and the S3 path
A Delta Sharing recipient, then a share containing the bucket path
A secret scope containing the AWS keys, then a notebook that mounts the bucket

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because cluster policies govern compute configuration; they do not authenticate to cloud storage or define allowed storage paths.

**B** is correct because Unity Catalog uses a two-layer model: a storage credential holds the cloud identity (IAM role or service principal), and an external location binds that credential to a specific cloud storage path prefix, together granting governed access.

**C** is incorrect because Delta Sharing is for sharing data with external recipients; it does not configure the Unity Catalog metastore's own access to cloud storage.

**D** is incorrect because storing raw AWS keys in secrets and using notebook mounts is the legacy pattern that Unity Catalog replaces; it bypasses Unity Catalog governance entirely.

[What is Unity Catalog?](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/)

questions.stem: A security team is implementing least-privilege access in Unity Catalog. Select TWO accurate statements about how privileges work. (Select TWO)

options.text: A user who is GRANTed SELECT on a schema automatically inherits SELECT on all tables within that schema
Table owners can always read their own table even without an explicit SELECT privilege
The USAGE privilege on a catalog is required before a user can query any table inside that catalog
Cluster policies replace GRANT statements and fully control what data a user can access

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because in Unity Catalog, granting a privilege on a container (schema) does NOT automatically grant it on all child objects; each object requires an explicit grant or the grant must be made at the catalog level with inheritance.

**B** is correct because the owner of a securable object in Unity Catalog has all privileges on it by default, including SELECT, without needing an explicit GRANT.

**C** is correct because Unity Catalog requires USAGE on every level of the hierarchy (catalog → schema) before a user can access objects within; missing USAGE at any level blocks access even if SELECT is granted on the table.

**D** is incorrect because cluster policies govern cluster configuration options (node types, autoscale limits, etc.) and are entirely separate from data access controls managed by Unity Catalog GRANT statements.

[Manage privileges in Unity Catalog](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/manage-privileges/)

questions.stem: A data engineer modifies the schema of a source Delta table in Unity Catalog. A manager asks which downstream tables and dashboards will be impacted. Which Unity Catalog feature MOST directly answers this?

options.text: Delta table DESCRIBE HISTORY, which shows which users committed changes to the table
VACUUM history, which records which files were removed and when
Cluster autoscaling metrics, which show which workloads were running at the time of the change
Data lineage, which shows column-level and table-level upstream and downstream dependencies

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Unity Catalog automatically captures data lineage at the column and table level across notebooks, jobs, and SQL queries, enabling the engineer to navigate downstream to all tables and dashboards that read from the modified source.

**B** is incorrect because VACUUM history records file-level garbage collection and retention; it has no knowledge of which downstream consumers depend on a table.

**C** is incorrect because autoscaling metrics capture compute utilization and do not describe data dependencies between datasets or dashboards.

**D** is incorrect because DESCRIBE HISTORY shows who wrote to the source table and what operations were performed, but does not identify downstream consumers of that table.

[Data lineage in Unity Catalog](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/data-lineage)

questions.stem: A job runs successfully in development but fails in production with `ModuleNotFoundError` for a third-party Python package. What is the BEST long-term fix?

options.text: Install the package interactively on the production cluster after each failure
Paste the package source code directly into the production notebook
Declare the dependency in the job or bundle configuration so it is installed automatically on job cluster creation
Lower the production driver node memory to match the development environment

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because interactive installation is manual, does not persist across cluster restarts, and must be repeated every time the job cluster is created — it is not a durable fix.

**B** is incorrect because vendoring raw library source into a notebook is fragile, prevents version management, and creates licensing and security concerns.

**C** is correct because declaring the library in the job task's libraries configuration (or in the bundle YAML) ensures the job cluster automatically installs the correct package version on every run, making the environment reproducible across development and production.

**D** is incorrect because the error is a missing Python module, not an out-of-memory error; node memory size is unrelated to package availability.

[CI/CD on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/dev-tools/ci-cd/)

questions.stem: A data engineering team wants automated test coverage for their Spark transformation logic before each production deployment. Which approach BEST aligns with Databricks CI/CD guidance?

options.text: Run OPTIMIZE on the output tables after each deployment to validate data quality
Write pytest unit tests using a local Spark session or DatabricksConnect, asserting DataFrame schemas and row counts against expected fixtures
Review notebook HTML exports visually before each release
Run tests only in production after the deployment to reduce cluster costs

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because OPTIMIZE is a data layout maintenance command; it does not validate transformation correctness or test business logic.

**B** is correct because Databricks recommends modular, testable Python functions with pytest-based unit tests that use a local Spark session or Databricks Connect; schema and row-count assertions run fast in CI before any production deployment.

**C** is incorrect because visual review of notebook HTML exports is a manual, subjective process that does not catch logic regressions automatically and cannot be enforced as a CI gate.

**D** is incorrect because testing in production after deployment is too late; failures at that stage have already affected production users and data.

[What are Declarative Automation Bundles?](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/dev-tools/bundles/)

questions.stem: In a medallion architecture, a data engineer is deciding which layer should store deduplicated, validated, and type-cast business entities ready for use by multiple downstream teams. Which layer is correct?

options.text: Bronze — because it captures the raw source data with full fidelity
Gold — because only consumption-ready aggregates belong there
Silver — because it is the conformance layer where raw data is cleaned and standardized for reuse
Landing — because all schemas are validated at the point of arrival

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because bronze preserves raw, unmodified source data including duplicates, nulls, and original data types; it is intentionally not clean.

**B** is incorrect because gold contains domain-specific aggregations and business-metric tables built on top of the conformed silver data; it is not where shared entity models live.

**C** is correct because silver is the conformance layer: data is deduplicated, validated against a schema, cast to correct types, and enriched with standard business keys, making it suitable for reuse by multiple downstream gold tables and teams.

**D** is incorrect because the landing zone is a raw file drop zone, not a processing layer; no transformations or schema validation are applied there.

[Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/)

questions.stem: A gold-layer table needs to track the full history of customer status changes (e.g., active → suspended → active) for audit and regulatory reporting. Which modeling pattern is MOST appropriate?

options.text: SCD Type 1 — overwrite the current status so only the latest value is stored
SCD Type 2 — add effective_start, effective_end, and is_current columns to retain history as new rows
Run VACUUM with RETAIN 0 HOURS to preserve one version per customer
Partition the table by current status so historical states are isolated per partition

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because SCD Type 1 overwrites the existing row with the new value, destroying historical state — this violates the audit and regulatory requirement to preserve the full change history.

**B** is correct because SCD Type 2 inserts a new row for each state change with effective date range columns, preserving the complete audit trail while allowing point-in-time queries.

**C** is incorrect because VACUUM removes unreferenced data files; it has no concept of customer rows or business keys and cannot be used to implement any slowly changing dimension pattern.

**D** is incorrect because partitioning by current status creates a layout for performance, not for historical tracking; when a customer's status changes, you would need to move rows between partitions, which is not a supported ACID pattern for history.

[Upsert into a Delta Lake table using merge](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/merge)
questions.reference: [Upsert into a Delta Lake table using merge](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/merge)

questions.stem: A senior engineer is designing a metadata-driven Lakeflow pipeline that dynamically generates streaming tables for dozens of data sources based on a config table. Select TWO statements that BEST describe how this pattern works. (Select TWO)

options.text: A Python function can loop over config rows and use dlt.table() or @dlt.table decorators to register each dataset programmatically
Delta Sharing must be enabled on the pipeline for metadata-driven table generation to function
The config table can be read with spark.read inside the pipeline notebook to drive dynamic table creation
Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines only support hard-coded table definitions and cannot use runtime metadata

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the DLT Python API supports calling dlt.table() inside a Python loop, allowing the pipeline to register N tables from N configuration rows without N hard-coded decorators.

**B** is incorrect because Delta Sharing is a data-sharing protocol between organizations and has no relationship to how pipeline datasets are defined or generated within a single workspace.

**C** is correct because the pipeline Python notebook can use spark.read to query a Unity Catalog table containing source configurations at pipeline definition time, making the registered datasets data-driven.

**D** is incorrect because Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines support dynamic, metadata-driven table generation using the Python API; pure SQL definitions are static, but Python offers full programmatic control.

[Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines Python language reference](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/python-ref)

questions.stem: A Lakeflow pipeline definition spans multiple Python source files in a Git-backed project. Which configuration controls which source files the pipeline runtime loads?

options.text: Each table definition must be duplicated in a single monolithic notebook
The cluster's init script installs all pipeline source files at runtime
The pipeline's libraries configuration lists the notebooks or Python files that define the pipeline datasets
The Unity Catalog external location points to the pipeline source directory

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a Lakeflow pipeline definition includes a libraries array that lists the notebooks, Python files, or JAR files the pipeline engine loads and executes; multiple files can be listed to span the pipeline across a modular project structure.

**B** is incorrect because init scripts run during cluster initialization to install software dependencies; they are not used to register pipeline dataset definitions.

**C** is incorrect because Lakeflow explicitly supports multi-file pipeline projects; monolithic notebooks are not required and are discouraged for large pipelines.

**D** is incorrect because external locations in Unity Catalog govern access to cloud storage paths for data; they are not used to reference pipeline source code.

[Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/)

questions.stem: A Python ETL function applies several business rules and returns a transformed DataFrame. How should this function be tested in a Databricks CI pipeline MOST efficiently?

options.text: Deploy the function to production and compare output counts in Databricks SQL
Write a pytest test that creates a small SparkSession, calls the function with a fixture DataFrame, and asserts the output schema and values
Run OPTIMIZE on the output table each night and monitor file counts as a proxy for correctness
Use a Databricks SQL alert to notify the team if row counts drop below a threshold after each job run

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because deploying to production first inverts the testing pipeline; failures affect live data and users before any validation occurs.

**B** is correct because a pytest test with a local or lightweight SparkSession runs in CI without a Databricks cluster, validates the function in isolation with controlled input data, and fails fast when business logic is broken.

**C** is incorrect because OPTIMIZE tracks file layout metrics, not business logic correctness; a buggy function that produces wrong values would still produce the same file count.

**D** is incorrect because a SQL alert based on row count is a production monitoring signal, not a pre-deployment correctness test; it cannot catch logic errors before they reach production.

[CI/CD on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/dev-tools/ci-cd/)

questions.stem: Auto Loader is ingesting JSON files from S3. After several months, new fields appear in the source files that were not present initially. Which Auto Loader feature handles this without pipeline failure?

options.text: DEEP CLONE — copies the schema from the source bucket into the Delta table automatically
VACUUM — removes old schema versions from the checkpoint so the new schema takes effect
Schema evolution — Auto Loader detects new columns and can be configured to merge them into the inferred schema
Liquid clustering — reorganizes columns in the file layout to accommodate new fields

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Auto Loader supports schema evolution: when cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode is set to addNewColumns (or similar modes), it detects new fields in arriving files and merges them into the stored schema, allowing the pipeline to continue without manual intervention.

**B** is incorrect because VACUUM is a file-cleanup command for Delta tables; it has no role in schema management or checkpoint evolution.

**C** is incorrect because DEEP CLONE replicates a Delta table's data and schema to another location; it does not infer or propagate schema changes from raw source files.

**D** is incorrect because liquid clustering is a data layout optimization technique that groups rows by clustering keys; it does not handle schema evolution.

[What is Auto Loader?](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/ingestion/cloud-object-storage/auto-loader/)

questions.stem: A team needs to ingest data from a REST API that returns paginated JSON responses and load it into a Delta table. The source has no native Databricks connector. Which approach BEST fits this scenario?

options.text: Use COPY INTO to load data directly from the REST API URL into Delta
Use Auto Loader's cloudFiles source to directly stream from the REST API endpoint
Configure Lakehouse Federation to register the REST API as a foreign catalog
Write a Python task in a Databricks job that calls the API, paginates through responses, assembles a DataFrame, and writes to Delta using foreachBatch or a batch write

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because for sources without a native connector, a custom Python task that calls the API, handles pagination, constructs a Spark DataFrame, and writes to Delta is the standard pattern on Databricks.

**B** is incorrect because Auto Loader's cloudFiles source reads files from cloud object storage (S3, ADLS, GCS); it cannot connect to arbitrary REST APIs.

**C** is incorrect because Lakehouse Federation supports specific relational database systems (PostgreSQL, MySQL, etc.) through JDBC-based connections; REST APIs are not supported foreign catalog sources.

**D** is incorrect because COPY INTO reads files from cloud storage paths, not from HTTP endpoints; it does not support REST API pagination.

[Lakeflow Jobs](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/jobs/

questions.stem: A Lakeflow pipeline expectation is defined with `ON VIOLATION DROP ROW`. What happens when a record fails the constraint?

options.text: The pipeline update fails immediately and requires manual restart
The violating record is excluded from the output dataset, a warning is logged, and violation counts are recorded in the event log
The record is quarantined to a system-managed _bad_records table in the same schema
The pipeline pauses until an operator reviews and approves the bad record

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because pipeline failure is the behavior of ON VIOLATION FAIL UPDATE, not DROP ROW; the DROP ROW mode is specifically designed to continue processing while filtering bad records.

**B** is correct because ON VIOLATION DROP ROW silently filters out records that fail the constraint, logs a warning-level event with the violation count, and records metrics in the pipeline event log — the pipeline continues normally.

**C** is incorrect because Lakeflow does not automatically create a separate quarantine table when using DROP ROW; the records are simply dropped. Engineers who want quarantine behavior must implement it manually using a separate table that captures the inverse of the expectation.

**D** is incorrect because Lakeflow pipelines are automated; they do not support interactive human approval gates within a running update.

[Manage data quality with pipeline expectations](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/expectations)

questions.stem: A team is building a multi-hop pipeline from bronze through gold. Select TWO statements that BEST describe correct medallion-layer responsibilities. (Select TWO)

options.text: Bronze should preserve the raw source record including bad rows, so data is never lost and issues can be reprocessed
Gold tables should contain raw, un-aggregated source data to give analysts maximum flexibility
Silver tables should enforce data types, remove duplicates, and apply standardized business keys
Running OPTIMIZE on bronze tables satisfies the silver-layer data quality requirements

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the bronze layer is an immutable landing zone for raw data; preserving bad rows allows engineers to debug source issues and reprocess data without going back to the source system.

**B** is incorrect because gold tables are designed for curated, domain-specific consumption — aggregates, metrics, wide tables — not raw data. Putting raw data in gold defeats the purpose of the medallion architecture.

**C** is correct because silver applies technical data quality: type casting, deduplication, null handling, and standardized key columns, producing a clean conformed dataset that multiple gold tables can safely build on.

**D** is incorrect because OPTIMIZE compacts file layout for read performance; it does not validate, cast, or deduplicate data and has nothing to do with silver-layer data quality logic.

[Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/)

questions.stem: A Delta Sharing provider publishes a share containing a Delta table. The provider later runs UPDATE statements on the table to fix bad data. What does the recipient observe?

options.text: The recipient's snapshot is frozen at the version when the share was first accepted and never changes
The recipient must delete and re-accept the share invitation to receive the updated data
The recipient's copy of the data is automatically updated because Delta Sharing reads from the provider's live table files
The provider must run DEEP CLONE after each update to push new files to the recipient

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Delta Sharing does not copy data files; instead, the provider grants the recipient time-limited pre-signed URLs to the provider's actual data files. When the provider updates the table, subsequent queries by the recipient read the updated files, so the recipient always sees current data.

**B** is incorrect because the share invitation is a one-time activation; recipients do not need to re-accept a share to see updated data — their queries automatically resolve to the latest table version.

**C** is incorrect because Delta Sharing is not a snapshot mechanism; it provides live access to the provider's table at query time.

**D** is incorrect because DEEP CLONE copies data to a destination Delta table and is unrelated to Delta Sharing; the Delta Sharing protocol does not require any additional data movement after updates.

[Create shares for OpenSharing](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-sharing/create-share)

questions.stem: A Lakehouse Federation foreign catalog has been registered in Unity Catalog for a MySQL source. Which statement about querying this foreign catalog is TRUE?

options.text: Queries against the foreign catalog copy all data into a Delta cache table before returning results
Queries against the foreign catalog are pushed down to the MySQL source and results are returned without permanent data movement
The foreign catalog can only be queried using the Delta Sharing protocol
Unity Catalog privileges do not apply to foreign catalogs; access is controlled entirely by MySQL user grants

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because Lakehouse Federation is designed for federated query without data movement; it does not create or populate a cache Delta table — results come directly from the source database.

**B** is correct because Lakehouse Federation pushes queries down to the connected external database, returns results to Databricks SQL, and does not persistently copy data into the lakehouse.

**C** is incorrect because foreign catalogs registered via Lakehouse Federation are queried directly through Databricks SQL or notebooks, not through the Delta Sharing protocol.

**D** is incorrect because Unity Catalog governs access to foreign catalogs using the same GRANT/REVOKE model as native catalogs; users still need appropriate Unity Catalog privileges to query foreign tables.

[Unity Catalog privileges reference](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/manage-privileges/privileges)

questions.stem: A Lakeflow pipeline event log shows a sudden spike in records dropped by an expectation named `non_null_customer_id`. What is the MOST appropriate first step to investigate?

options.text: Run OPTIMIZE on the pipeline output table to reorganize files and see if the spike disappears
Query the event log to find the batch timestamp and source files associated with the spike, then inspect the raw bronze data for the corresponding window
Delete the expectation from the pipeline definition and redeploy to stop the alerts
Increase the cluster size so the pipeline processes records faster and reduces the violation window

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because OPTIMIZE compacts files for read performance; it has no effect on expectation violation counts and cannot help identify why null customer IDs appeared.

**B** is correct because the Lakeflow event log records per-update expectation metrics including violated record counts; correlating the spike timestamp with source files and bronze data allows the engineer to find the upstream system change or bad data batch that caused the issue.

**C** is incorrect because removing the expectation hides the problem rather than fixing it; null customer IDs would then silently propagate into silver and gold tables.

**D** is incorrect because expectation violations are driven by data quality, not processing speed; a larger cluster processes the same bad data faster but does not fix the source issue.

[Manage data quality with pipeline expectations](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/expectations)

questions.stem: A streaming job writes to Delta using foreachBatch. The team wants an alert when the silver table has not received a new commit in more than 30 minutes, indicating the stream has stalled. Which approach BEST implements this?

options.text: Set an autoscaling policy on the streaming cluster to trigger a notification when cluster size reaches maximum
Run VACUUM every 30 minutes and alert when VACUUM finds no files to delete
Create a Databricks SQL alert on a query that checks MAX(commit_timestamp) from the table's DESCRIBE HISTORY and alerts when it exceeds 30 minutes ago
Use liquid clustering on the silver table; a stalled stream will cause clustering to degrade, triggering an alert

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because DESCRIBE HISTORY returns a timestamp for each Delta table commit; a SQL alert that queries MAX(timestamp) from the history and fires when it is more than 30 minutes old detects a stalled stream with zero additional infrastructure.

**B** is incorrect because VACUUM removes old unreferenced files; the presence or absence of files to vacuum has no relationship to whether the streaming job is actively writing new data.

**C** is incorrect because autoscaling notifications reflect compute utilization, not data pipeline progress; a stalled stream might leave the cluster idle at minimum nodes without any alert firing.

**D** is incorrect because liquid clustering improves file layout for read performance; it does not monitor or respond to streaming activity, and there is no mechanism by which a stalled stream causes clustering degradation alerts.

[Remove unused data files with vacuum](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/vacuum)

questions.stem: After running OPTIMIZE on a large Delta table, a data engineer wants to remove data files that are no longer referenced by the Delta log. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: Drop and recreate the table to eliminate the old files
Run OPTIMIZE again to overwrite the old files with new compacted ones
Use RESTORE to roll back to the version before OPTIMIZE was run
VACUUM the table to delete files older than the retention threshold that are no longer in the active Delta log

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because VACUUM removes data files that are no longer referenced in the Delta transaction log and are older than the configured retention period (default 7 days), reclaiming storage after OPTIMIZE creates replacement compacted files.

**B** is incorrect because running OPTIMIZE again rewrites active data files but leaves the previous set of unreferenced files in storage until VACUUM is run.

**C** is incorrect because RESTORE rolls back the table to a prior version, undoing the OPTIMIZE, which is the opposite of what is needed.

**D** is incorrect because dropping and recreating the table is a destructive operation that deletes all data and history; it is not the appropriate tool for routine storage cleanup.

[Remove unused data files with vacuum](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/vacuum)

questions.stem: A team sets VACUUM RETAIN 0 HOURS on a production Delta table to aggressively reclaim storage. What risk does this introduce?

options.text: The table's active data will be permanently deleted because VACUUM ignores the Delta log when retention is 0
Any concurrent readers using snapshot isolation may fail because the files they reference could be deleted mid-query
OPTIMIZE will no longer work on the table because VACUUM cleared the file-size metadata
Unity Catalog lineage tracking will stop for the table because VACUUM removes the audit log

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because VACUUM only removes files that are no longer referenced in the Delta log; it does not delete the active version of the data even at 0-hour retention.

**B** is correct because with zero retention, VACUUM can delete files that were part of a prior version while a long-running reader is still using them under snapshot isolation; those readers will fail with file-not-found errors mid-query — this is why Databricks requires disabling a safety check before allowing sub-7-day retention.

**C** is incorrect because OPTIMIZE reads the Delta log to identify active data files and rewrite them; VACUUM does not touch the Delta log or remove file-size metadata that OPTIMIZE uses.

**D** is incorrect because Unity Catalog lineage is captured at query execution time in separate audit log storage; VACUUM on a data table does not affect lineage records.

[Remove unused data files with vacuum](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/vacuum)

questions.stem: A data engineering team wants to reduce the DBU cost of their nightly batch pipeline that processes a large Delta table. Select TWO approaches that are MOST likely to reduce cost without compromising correctness. (Select TWO)

options.text: Switch from an all-purpose interactive cluster to a job cluster that terminates immediately after the job completes
Enable Photon on the job cluster to accelerate vectorized execution and reduce wall-clock time
Disable checkpointing to avoid checkpoint write overhead
Increase the number of shuffle partitions to the maximum supported value to maximize parallelism

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because all-purpose clusters accrue DBUs even when idle; job clusters are provisioned just-in-time and terminated on completion, eliminating idle cost.

**B** is correct because Photon-accelerated clusters process data faster, reducing the wall-clock runtime; since DBU charges are time-based, shorter runtime on the same node type reduces total cost.

**C** is incorrect because disabling checkpointing removes fault tolerance; a mid-job failure requires a full rerun from scratch, which can dramatically increase total cost and is never recommended for production pipelines.

**D** is incorrect because setting shuffle partitions to the maximum creates excessive task overhead (too many tiny tasks) and typically increases runtime and cost rather than reducing it; the optimal value depends on data volume.

[Lakeflow Jobs](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/jobs/)

questions.stem: A compliance team requires that all access to a sensitive Unity Catalog table containing PII be logged for audit. Which mechanism provides this?

options.text: A Databricks SQL alert on row count, which fires whenever a user reads more than 1000 rows
The Delta table HISTORY, which records every user who ran a SELECT query against the table
The cluster event log, which records all SQL statements executed on the cluster
The Unity Catalog audit log, which records all data access events including SELECT queries, table reads, and privilege changes

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Unity Catalog emits audit log events for all data access operations — including SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and privilege changes — to a configurable audit log destination (system tables or external storage), satisfying compliance requirements.

**B** is incorrect because Delta HISTORY records write operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, OPTIMIZE, VACUUM) committed to the table; it does not log read-only SELECT queries by users.

**C** is incorrect because the cluster event log records cluster lifecycle events (start, resize, terminate) and driver output; it does not capture individual SQL queries executed on the cluster.

**D** is incorrect because a SQL alert on row count is a threshold notification, not an audit log; it does not record who accessed the table or what data they read.

[What is Unity Catalog?](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/)

questions.stem: A Unity Catalog admin wants to apply a tag to a Delta table column to mark it as containing PII. What is the MOST direct way to do this in Databricks?

options.text: Rename the column to include _pii suffix so downstream consumers know it is sensitive
Add a comment to the column using ALTER COLUMN ... COMMENT and prefix it with PII:
Create a separate audit table that maps column names to PII classification labels
Use ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... SET TAGS to apply a key-value tag to the column in Unity Catalog

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Unity Catalog supports attribute tagging at the catalog, schema, table, and column level; ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... SET TAGS is the native SQL syntax for attaching key-value tags that are discoverable in the Unity Catalog UI and queryable via system tables.

**B** is incorrect because column comments are free-text descriptions; they are not structured metadata that governance tools can programmatically query or filter by classification type.

**C** is incorrect because a separate audit table is a manual workaround that requires custom tooling to maintain and is not integrated with Unity Catalog's built-in governance features.

**D** is incorrect because column name conventions are fragile, not enforced by any governance layer, and require renaming columns in all downstream queries.

[Data lineage in Unity Catalog](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/data-lineage)

questions.stem: A Lakeflow pipeline fails with `AnalysisException: Table or view not found` for a dataset that is referenced in the pipeline but defined in a different source file. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: The pipeline does not have a checkpoint directory configured
The Delta table was deleted by a VACUUM command before the pipeline could read it
The pipeline's libraries configuration does not include the source file that defines the missing dataset
The Unity Catalog schema was not granted USAGE privilege to the pipeline service principal

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Lakeflow only registers datasets defined in the source files listed in the pipeline's libraries array; if the file containing a referenced dataset is missing from that list, the pipeline's dependency resolver cannot find the definition and raises AnalysisException.

**B** is incorrect because VACUUM removes unreferenced data files, not the table itself; the table definition in the metastore remains and the AnalysisException would have a different message if data files were missing.

**C** is incorrect because the absence of a checkpoint directory causes a streaming pipeline to start from the beginning on restart; it does not cause a dataset-not-found error.

**D** is incorrect because a USAGE privilege issue produces an access denied or permission error, not a table-not-found error.

[Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/)

questions.stem: A Databricks Asset Bundle is deployed to production but the job fails because the target catalog specified in the bundle does not exist in the production workspace. Which bundle feature prevents this class of error before deployment?

options.text: Setting the streaming checkpoint to a local path so the job falls back to a local catalog
Running OPTIMIZE on the target catalog tables before deployment
Enabling Photon on the production job cluster to catch runtime configuration errors faster
Running `databricks bundle validate` to parse and validate the YAML configuration before deploying

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `databricks bundle validate` parses the bundle YAML, resolves variable substitutions, and checks for configuration errors without making any changes to the workspace; catching a misconfigured catalog name here avoids a failed production deployment.

**B** is incorrect because OPTIMIZE is a Delta maintenance command; it has no ability to validate bundle YAML configuration or check whether catalogs exist.

**C** is incorrect because Photon accelerates SQL execution and does not perform configuration validation or catch missing Unity Catalog objects before a job starts.

**D** is incorrect because the checkpoint path stores streaming offset state; it has no relationship to catalog configuration and cannot act as a fallback for a missing Unity Catalog catalog.

[What are Declarative Automation Bundles?](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/dev-tools/bundles/)

questions.stem: A team models a products dimension table using SCD Type 2 in Delta Lake. Product prices change frequently. An analyst queries the table to find the price of a product as it was on a specific historical date. Which query pattern uses the SCD Type 2 columns correctly?

options.text: SELECT price FROM products WHERE product_id = 'X' AND is_current = true
SELECT price FROM products WHERE product_id = 'X' AND '2024-03-15' BETWEEN effective_start AND effective_end
SELECT price FROM products VERSION AS OF 42 WHERE product_id = 'X'
SELECT price FROM products WHERE product_id = 'X' ORDER BY effective_start DESC LIMIT 1

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because filtering on is_current = true returns the latest price, not the price as of a specific historical date; this query is correct for current-state lookups only.

**B** is correct because SCD Type 2 stores each version as a row with effective_start and effective_end date boundaries; querying WHERE '2024-03-15' BETWEEN effective_start AND effective_end returns exactly the row that was active on that date.

**C** is incorrect because Delta's VERSION AS OF syntax uses the table's transaction log version number, not a business date; these versions correspond to Delta commits, not SCD effective dates.

**D** is incorrect because ORDER BY effective_start DESC LIMIT 1 returns the most recently effective row, which is equivalent to the current row — not the historically correct one for a given date.

[Upsert into a Delta Lake table using merge](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/merge)
questions.reference: [Upsert into a Delta Lake table using merge](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/merge)

questions.stem: A sales analytics gold table is structured as a star schema. The team debates whether to use wide denormalized tables or normalized dimension tables. Which consideration MOST favors wide denormalized gold tables on Databricks?

options.text: Wide tables are required because Delta Lake does not support foreign key constraints between dimension and fact tables
Wide tables use less storage than normalized dimension tables because each attribute appears only once
Normalized dimension tables are faster for BI dashboards because they avoid full table scans
Wide tables reduce join complexity for BI tools, and columnar storage in Delta Lake means unused columns are not read when only a subset of columns is queried

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Databricks Delta Lake uses columnar Parquet files; queries projecting a subset of columns skip the irrelevant column data entirely. Wide denormalized tables eliminate join overhead for BI tools that typically select a handful of columns, making them well-suited for the gold layer.

**B** is incorrect because wide denormalized tables store repeated dimensional attributes for every fact row, consuming more storage than normalized dimension tables where each attribute appears once.

**C** is incorrect because normalized dimension tables require joins at query time; in a BI workload with millions of fact rows, these joins add overhead compared to pre-joined wide tables.

**D** is incorrect because while Delta Lake does not enforce referential integrity like a relational database, this is not the reason to choose wide tables; the choice is driven by query performance and simplicity, not by a technical constraint.

[Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/)

questions.stem: A team is migrating notebook-based ETL pipelines to Databricks Asset Bundles. Select TWO capabilities of bundles that most improve deployment governance. (Select TWO)

options.text: Bundles support per-environment variable overrides so dev, staging, and prod can use different catalog names or cluster sizes from a single source
Bundles automatically compress all Delta tables in the deployment target to reduce storage cost
Bundles can be validated with `databricks bundle validate` before any resources are deployed, catching configuration errors in CI
Bundles replace Unity Catalog as the primary access control mechanism for deployed jobs

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because bundle targets allow per-environment variable substitution in YAML, enabling a single bundle definition to deploy identically structured resources across dev, staging, and prod environments with environment-specific overrides.

**B** is incorrect because bundles manage resource definitions and deployments; they do not perform Delta table operations like compaction or OPTIMIZE on tables in the deployment target.

**C** is correct because `databricks bundle validate` is a client-side YAML validation step that parses the bundle configuration and checks for errors without creating any workspace resources, making it suitable as a CI gate.

**D** is incorrect because Unity Catalog remains the access control layer for data objects; bundles are a deployment tool for workspace resources (jobs, pipelines, clusters) and do not replace or interact with Unity Catalog privilege management.

[What are Declarative Automation Bundles?](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/dev-tools/bundles/)

questions.stem: A Lakeflow streaming pipeline must implement exactly-once semantics when writing to a Delta table using foreachBatch. Which combination is required?

options.text: Using AUTO OPTIMIZE to merge micro-batch files after each write
Disabling the Delta write-ahead log to allow faster writes without transaction overhead
Configuring the sink to use CSV format with an append mode
A stable checkpoint location and idempotent writes using MERGE or replaceWhere in the foreachBatch function

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because foreachBatch can be called multiple times for the same batch ID on retry; combining a stable checkpoint (so Structured Streaming tracks committed batches) with idempotent write logic (MERGE on a unique key or replaceWhere on the batch partition) ensures that a retried batch produces the same result without duplicates.

**B** is incorrect because Delta's write-ahead log is what provides ACID guarantees and enables exactly-once semantics; disabling it would remove the foundation of transactional writes.

**C** is incorrect because CSV format is not transactional; appending to CSV on a retry would create duplicate rows with no mechanism to detect or remove them.

**D** is incorrect because AUTO OPTIMIZE (auto compaction and optimized writes) is a background file management feature; it does not provide idempotency or exactly-once guarantees for streaming writes.

[Structured Streaming checkpoints](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/structured-streaming/checkpoints)

questions.stem: A pipeline processes events from 50 source tables using identical transformation logic. To avoid 50 copies of the same code, an engineer wraps the logic in a Python function and calls it dynamically. When is this function registered in the DLT graph?

options.text: After the first OPTIMIZE runs on the output table, completing the graph registration
At query time, when each downstream table first references the transformed dataset
When the cluster starts, before the pipeline definition is parsed
At pipeline update time, when DLT evaluates the pipeline source code and calls the function for each configured source to register the resulting dataset nodes

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because DLT evaluates the Python pipeline source code during the graph planning phase of each pipeline update; any calls to dlt.table() or uses of the @dlt.table decorator inside the function are executed at this time to register all dataset nodes before data processing begins.

**B** is incorrect because DLT resolves the full dependency graph before processing any data; it does not lazily register datasets on first reference from a downstream consumer.

**C** is incorrect because pipeline graph planning happens after cluster startup as part of the pipeline update lifecycle, not during the cluster initialization phase.

**D** is incorrect because OPTIMIZE is a post-write maintenance command; the DLT graph is fully resolved before any data is written and is completely independent of OPTIMIZE.

[Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines Python language reference](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/python-ref)

questions.stem: A team is evaluating Auto Loader versus a scheduled COPY INTO for ingesting Parquet files from ADLS Gen2. Select TWO TRUE statements that differentiate the two approaches. (Select TWO)

options.text: Auto Loader maintains a stateful checkpoint of processed files and supports continuous streaming mode, while COPY INTO uses an idempotent file-tracking mechanism suitable for triggered batch runs
COPY INTO supports schema evolution via cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode, while Auto Loader requires a static schema
Auto Loader scales to millions of files through file notification or directory listing modes, while COPY INTO lists files on every run and can become slow with very large file counts
COPY INTO requires a Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline, while Auto Loader can be used in any notebook or job

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Auto Loader is built on Structured Streaming with persistent checkpoints and supports triggered and continuous modes, while COPY INTO tracks which files have been loaded in a per-table internal state, making it suitable for batch-triggered ingestion.

**B** is incorrect because it reverses the capabilities: Auto Loader is the feature that supports cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode for automatic schema inference and evolution; COPY INTO requires the schema to be defined or inferred statically at execution time.

**C** is correct because Auto Loader's file notification mode (via cloud event notifications) scales efficiently to millions of files without listing the directory; COPY INTO uses directory listing on each execution, which becomes slow at very large file counts.

**D** is incorrect because COPY INTO is a SQL command that runs in any notebook, job, or SQL warehouse; it does not require a Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline.

[What is Auto Loader?](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/ingestion/cloud-object-storage/auto-loader/)

questions.stem: A team sets cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode to `rescue` in their Auto Loader pipeline. What happens when a new column arrives in an incoming file that is not in the current schema?

options.text: The pipeline fails with a schema mismatch error and must be restarted with the updated schema
The new column's data is captured in a special _rescued_data JSON column rather than being dropped or causing a failure
The new column is automatically added to the target Delta table schema and written as a regular column
The file containing the new column is skipped and moved to a quarantine path

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the rescue mode is specifically designed to avoid pipeline failures on schema changes; it accommodates unexpected columns gracefully.

**B** is correct because with rescue mode, columns not present in the current schema are serialized into the _rescued_data string column as JSON, preserving the data without schema evolution and without dropping or losing unexpected values.

**C** is incorrect because adding the new column as a regular column to the target table is the behavior of the addNewColumns or failOnNewColumns-disabled mode, not the rescue mode.

**D** is incorrect because Auto Loader does not have a built-in quarantine path mechanism for schema mismatches; files are not skipped or rerouted based on schema mode settings.

[What is Auto Loader?](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/ingestion/cloud-object-storage/auto-loader/)

questions.stem: A silver table must deduplicate events using dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark. The team sets the watermark to 1 hour. What limitation must they understand?

options.text: Duplicates arriving more than 1 hour after the first occurrence of the same key may not be deduplicated
The watermark applies to file sizes, so files larger than 1 hour of data will be dropped
dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark requires the output table to be partitioned by hour to function
The 1-hour watermark means the pipeline will only run once per hour in trigger-available-now mode

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark maintains a state store of keys seen within the watermark window; once the watermark advances past a key's event time, that key is evicted from state. A late-arriving duplicate more than 1 hour behind the watermark will not be matched against the evicted state entry and will pass through as a new row.

**B** is incorrect because watermarks are event-time thresholds for late data handling in Structured Streaming; they have no relationship to file sizes.

**C** is incorrect because dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark does not require any specific table partition layout; it operates on streaming state, not on the physical table organization.

**D** is incorrect because the watermark controls when state is evicted, not the trigger interval; trigger.availableNow processes all available data in one or more micro-batches regardless of the watermark duration.

[Structured Streaming concepts](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/structured-streaming/)

questions.stem: A MERGE statement is used to upsert CDC records into a Delta silver table. The source CDC stream contains rows with op_type values INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. The MERGE uses customer_id as the join key. Which MERGE clause handles physical deletions from the source correctly?

options.text: WHEN MATCHED AND source.op_type = 'DELETE' THEN DELETE
WHEN NOT MATCHED AND source.op_type = 'DELETE' THEN INSERT (customer_id) VALUES (source.customer_id)
WHEN MATCHED AND source.op_type = 'DELETE' THEN UPDATE SET is_deleted = true
Deletions are handled automatically by the MERGE statement without an explicit clause

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because WHEN MATCHED AND source.op_type = 'DELETE' THEN DELETE will physically remove the matching row from the target table when a CDC delete event arrives for an existing customer_id — this is the correct MERGE syntax for hard deletes.

**B** is incorrect because WHEN NOT MATCHED fires when the source key has no matching target row; inserting a delete event for a non-existent key would create a phantom row, the opposite of the desired behavior.

**C** is incorrect because SET is_deleted = true is a soft delete pattern; it marks the row rather than removing it. While valid for some use cases, it does not physically delete the row as the question requires.

**D** is incorrect because MERGE does not automatically infer intent from op_type column values; delete handling must be explicitly coded with an appropriate WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE clause.

[Upsert into a Delta Lake table using merge](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/merge)
questions.reference: [Upsert into a Delta Lake table using merge](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/merge)

questions.stem: A Unity Catalog admin wants to share data from a Delta table with a team at a subsidiary that uses Snowflake. The subsidiary does not have Databricks. Which Delta Sharing configuration is required on the Databricks side?

options.text: Export the Delta table to Parquet using COPY INTO and upload the files to Snowflake internal storage
Run DEEP CLONE to copy the Delta table to a Snowflake-accessible S3 prefix, then share the prefix via Snowflake external stage
Configure a Lakehouse Federation connection from Snowflake's side to read the Delta table directly
Create a share, add the table to the share, create a recipient with a Snowflake-specific token type, and activate the sharing link

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Delta Sharing (OpenSharing) protocol is open and platform-agnostic; the Databricks provider creates a share, adds the table, creates a recipient profile, and the Snowflake consumer uses the Delta Sharing connector to query the data without a Databricks account.

**B** is incorrect because DEEP CLONE copies data files and creates a static snapshot; this is data movement, not live sharing, and requires the admin to maintain the clone on every update.

**C** is incorrect because Lakehouse Federation is a Databricks feature for querying external databases from Databricks; it cannot be configured from Snowflake's side to read Databricks Delta tables.

**D** is incorrect because exporting to Parquet via COPY INTO is a one-time data movement that creates stale files; it loses all Delta Lake metadata and requires ongoing operational maintenance.

[Create shares for OpenSharing](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-sharing/create-share)

questions.stem: A data architect is choosing between Lakehouse Federation and replicating external data into Delta Lake. Select TWO scenarios where Lakehouse Federation is the BETTER choice. (Select TWO)

options.text: Ad-hoc analyst queries need real-time data from an operational PostgreSQL database and data latency must be under one minute
A machine learning pipeline reads a large fact table daily for training and requires maximum read throughput with Photon acceleration
A compliance report joins a Unity Catalog Delta table with current data from a MySQL source, and the report is run on demand
A streaming pipeline processes high-volume clickstream data and writes enriched events to a gold Delta table for real-time dashboards

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Lakehouse Federation provides near-real-time access to live operational data without replication lag; for sub-minute freshness requirements this is better than a scheduled ETL pipeline.

**B** is incorrect because Lakehouse Federation pushes queries to the source database, which lacks Photon acceleration and columnar scan optimization; replicating into Delta Lake and running Photon-accelerated queries there is much faster for large ML training reads.

**C** is correct because a join between Unity Catalog and a MySQL foreign catalog is a natural Lakehouse Federation use case — the analyst gets current MySQL data in the same SQL query without an ETL pipeline.

**D** is incorrect because Lakehouse Federation is designed for read-only federated queries, not for high-volume streaming ingestion; a streaming pipeline that writes to a gold table should use Auto Loader or Kafka source for ingestion, not a federated connection.

[Unity Catalog privileges reference](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/manage-privileges/privileges)

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to set up an on-call alert when a production Databricks job fails. Which built-in Databricks feature MOST directly supports this?

options.text: Configure a job notification on the job definition to send an email or webhook on failure
Set a Delta table retention period shorter than the job SLA so VACUUM alerts the team when it runs
Create a Databricks SQL alert that queries the Delta output table row count hourly
Enable liquid clustering on the output table so failed writes generate a cluster-level alert

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Databricks Jobs natively support notifications (email or webhook to PagerDuty, Slack, etc.) on job start, success, failure, and other events; this is the built-in, zero-configuration-overhead way to get on-call alerts for production job failures.

**B** is incorrect because VACUUM is a maintenance command; it has no mechanism to detect job failures or send notifications.

**C** is incorrect because a SQL alert on row count detects data quality anomalies, not job execution failures; a failed job that writes zero rows would trigger the alert, but a job that fails before writing anything might not update the table at all.

**D** is incorrect because liquid clustering optimizes data file layout for read performance; it has no relationship to job failure detection or alerting.

[Lakeflow Jobs](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/jobs/)

questions.stem: A team wants to monitor the health of a Lakeflow pipeline. Select TWO metrics or artifacts that are natively available in the Lakeflow pipeline event log. (Select TWO)

options.text: Per-table expectation violation counts and rates for each pipeline update
The number of Parquet files compacted by OPTIMIZE during each pipeline run
The number of output rows written to each dataset in the pipeline
The cloud billing cost in USD for each pipeline update

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the pipeline event log (the system table event_log) records expectation metrics for each update, including the number of passed, failed, and dropped records per expectation per dataset.

**B** is incorrect because the pipeline event log does not record OPTIMIZE file compaction metrics; OPTIMIZE is a separate maintenance operation whose metrics appear in the Delta table's DESCRIBE HISTORY output.

**C** is correct because the pipeline event log records flow progress metrics including num_output_rows for each dataset, enabling engineers to monitor data volume flowing through the pipeline.

**D** is incorrect because cloud billing cost is not captured in the pipeline event log; DBU consumption is tracked in the Databricks account console and billing APIs, not in the pipeline's own event log.

[Manage data quality with pipeline expectations](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/expectations)

questions.stem: A Databricks SQL warehouse is used exclusively for scheduled dashboard refreshes that run between midnight and 6 AM. The warehouse sits idle the rest of the day. Which configuration MOST reduces cost?

options.text: Set the warehouse auto-stop timeout to 10 minutes so it stops automatically when idle
Increase the warehouse size to XXL so each dashboard refresh completes faster
Disable query result caching so each refresh runs fresh queries
Switch from serverless to classic warehouse type to avoid per-second billing

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because with a 10-minute auto-stop timeout, the SQL warehouse terminates shortly after the 6 AM refresh completes; it is not running (and not incurring DBU cost) during the 18 hours of inactivity each day.

**B** is incorrect because upgrading to a larger warehouse size reduces query duration but increases the DBU rate per second; the 18 idle hours dominate total cost, so size increases make costs worse if the warehouse is not properly auto-stopped.

**C** is incorrect because disabling query result caching forces recomputation on every refresh, increasing runtime and cost; caching reduces cost by avoiding redundant computation.

**D** is incorrect because serverless warehouses can start faster and stop within seconds of inactivity; switching to classic warehouses typically has longer start times and the same idle-time billing issue.

[Lakeflow Jobs](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/jobs/)

questions.stem: A job reads a Delta table, applies a window function partitioned by customer_id, and writes results. The query uses 200 default shuffle partitions but customer_id cardinality is 5 million. What tuning change is MOST likely to improve performance?

options.text: Increase spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to a value closer to the number of distinct customer_id values so each partition handles a manageable subset of customers
Run OPTIMIZE on the output table before the job starts to improve write performance
Replace the window function with a GROUP BY and aggregate to avoid shuffle entirely
Enable AUTO OPTIMIZE on the source table to speed up the read phase of the job

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because with 5 million distinct customer_id values and only 200 shuffle partitions, each shuffle partition must handle approximately 25,000 customers, leading to large individual partition sizes and spill. Increasing shuffle partitions (e.g., to 2000-5000) distributes the load more evenly and reduces per-partition memory pressure.

**B** is incorrect because OPTIMIZE compacts the output table's existing data files and has no effect on the source read or window computation performance.

**C** is incorrect because window functions and GROUP BY aggregations serve different purposes; replacing a window function with GROUP BY changes the query semantics and may produce incorrect results if the original logic requires per-row context within a group.

**D** is incorrect because AUTO OPTIMIZE manages background file compaction for write performance; it does not accelerate scan or shuffle operations during a job run.

[Optimize data file layout](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/optimize)

questions.stem: A data engineer grants a group SELECT on a gold table containing PII. The security team later requires that SSN values be masked for all non-privileged users. Which Unity Catalog feature implements this WITHOUT changing the underlying data?

options.text: Apply a column mask function using ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... SET MASK to return a masked value for non-privileged users
Run UPDATE on the gold table to hash all SSN values permanently
