Create a view that calls SHA256(ssn) and revoke SELECT on the base table for non-privileged users
Enable Delta table encryption so SSN column data is encrypted at rest and unreadable by non-privileged users

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Unity Catalog column masks allow a SQL function to be attached to a column; non-privileged users who query the table see the masked value (e.g., 'XXX-XX-XXXX') while privileged users see the real value, without modifying the stored data.

**B** is incorrect because permanently hashing SSN values in the table modifies the underlying data, is irreversible, and violates the requirement to leave the underlying data unchanged.

**C** is incorrect because creating a view with hashing does implement masking, but it is more complex to maintain than a column mask, requires revoking base table access, and can be bypassed if the base table access is not fully revoked; the column mask approach is the preferred Unity Catalog native solution.

**D** is incorrect because Delta table encryption (at-rest encryption via cloud KMS) is storage-level encryption managed by the cloud provider; it encrypts all data equally and does not provide column-level, user-context-aware masking.

[Manage privileges in Unity Catalog](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/manage-privileges/)

questions.stem: A workspace admin needs to ensure that all users in the `analysts` group can only query tables in the `prod.reporting` schema and no other schema. Which set of grants is SUFFICIENT?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON CATALOG prod TO analysts; GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA prod.reporting TO analysts; GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA prod.reporting TO analysts
GRANT SELECT ON CATALOG prod TO analysts
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON SCHEMA prod.reporting TO analysts
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA prod.reporting TO analysts

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Unity Catalog requires USAGE at every level of the hierarchy before a user can access an object; USAGE on the catalog allows navigation into it, USAGE on the schema allows navigation into the schema, and SELECT on the schema grants read access to all current and future tables within it.

**B** is incorrect because GRANT SELECT ON CATALOG gives analysts SELECT access to every schema in the prod catalog, not just prod.reporting — this violates the least-privilege requirement.

**C** is incorrect because GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES includes CREATE, MODIFY, and other write privileges in addition to SELECT; granting all privileges to analysts violates the principle of least privilege for a read-only use case.

**D** is incorrect because USAGE alone on the schema allows navigation but does not grant SELECT; analysts would be denied when trying to actually query any tables.

[Unity Catalog privileges reference](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/manage-privileges/privileges)

questions.stem: A company policy requires that any time a Delta table containing PII is read, the event must be queryable from Databricks system tables within 15 minutes. Which feature supports this requirement?

options.text: Unity Catalog audit logs written to the system.access.audit system table, which captures data access events in near real-time
Delta table DESCRIBE HISTORY, which logs every SELECT query with the user identity and timestamp
Databricks SQL query history in the UI, which shows recent queries but does not write to a queryable system table
The cluster event log, which captures all Spark plan executions including reads from PII tables

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Unity Catalog emits audit events to the system.access.audit system table; this table is queryable from Databricks SQL and captures data access events including SELECT queries on Unity Catalog-governed tables with user, timestamp, and table information — enabling near-real-time compliance queries.

**B** is incorrect because Delta DESCRIBE HISTORY captures write operations committed to the table (MERGE, UPDATE, DELETE, OPTIMIZE); read-only SELECT queries do not create Delta log entries and therefore do not appear in DESCRIBE HISTORY.

**C** is incorrect because the Databricks SQL query history is a UI feature for interactive exploration; while it stores recent query text, it is not a system table accessible via SQL and does not have the structured audit trail that compliance requires.

**D** is incorrect because the cluster event log records cluster lifecycle events; it does not capture individual Spark SQL read operations or link them to Unity Catalog table identities.

[Data lineage in Unity Catalog](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/data-lineage)

questions.stem: A Lakeflow pipeline update fails with the error: `Flow 'enrich_orders' failed: Stream is stateful. Stateful streaming flows cannot be reset automatically.` Which action resolves this?

options.text: Run OPTIMIZE on the enrich_orders target table to repair the streaming state
Enable full refresh on the pipeline to reset the stateful stream's checkpoint and reprocess from source
Decrease the watermark duration on the stateful aggregation to clear the state store
Delete the pipeline and recreate it with a new pipeline ID to reset the checkpoint

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because OPTIMIZE is a file compaction command that operates on the target table's data files; it has no mechanism to interact with or repair Structured Streaming state stores.

**B** is correct because in Lakeflow, triggering a full refresh clears the pipeline's checkpoint and state stores, allowing stateful streaming flows to restart from the beginning of their source; this is the documented way to reset a stateful stream in a DLT pipeline.

**C** is incorrect because changing the watermark duration alters how long state is retained going forward; it does not resolve a corrupted or incompatible state store from a prior pipeline version.

**D** is incorrect because deleting and recreating the pipeline is unnecessarily destructive; the full refresh option on the existing pipeline achieves the same reset without losing the pipeline definition and configuration.

[Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-live-tables/)

questions.stem: A bundle deployment to staging succeeds but the same bundle fails in production. Select TWO investigation steps that are MOST likely to identify environment-specific configuration differences. (Select TWO)

options.text: Run `databricks bundle validate --target prod` to see the fully-rendered production configuration including variable substitutions
Run OPTIMIZE on the production output Delta tables to check if their schema matches staging
Compare the job run error message in the production job run against the staging run history to identify the first diverging step
Enable liquid clustering on the production cluster to improve state management

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `databricks bundle validate --target prod` renders the final YAML with all environment-specific variable substitutions applied, making it easy to spot configuration differences (wrong catalog name, missing secret scope, different cluster policy) without a live deployment.

**B** is incorrect because OPTIMIZE is a data maintenance operation; running it on output tables reveals nothing about why a deployment or job configuration failed.

**C** is correct because comparing the production error message with staging run history pinpoints the exact task, step, or configuration value that behaves differently between environments, narrowing the search to a specific failure cause.

**D** is incorrect because liquid clustering affects data file layout on tables; it has no relationship to job or bundle deployment configuration differences between environments.

[What are Declarative Automation Bundles?](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/dev-tools/bundles/)

questions.stem: A gold table storing daily sales aggregates is rebuilt in full every night using a complete overwrite. Which Delta Lake write pattern ensures the old data is atomically replaced and readers never see a partially written state?

options.text: Delete all rows with DELETE FROM, then insert new rows with INSERT INTO in two separate transactions
Write the new data with mode('overwrite') using a DataFrame write, which creates a new table version atomically
Drop the table and recreate it so readers can query it immediately after the CREATE TABLE statement
Use VACUUM with RETAIN 0 HOURS immediately before the write to clear old files and make room for new data

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because executing DELETE then INSERT as two separate transactions creates a window between the two commits during which the table appears empty to concurrent readers — this violates the atomicity requirement.

**B** is correct because Delta Lake's overwrite mode performs the entire operation as a single atomic transaction; the new version becomes visible only when the write completes successfully, ensuring readers never see a partial state.

**C** is incorrect because dropping and recreating a table is a two-step operation (DROP then CREATE) involving two separate commits; there is a window after the DROP during which the table does not exist, causing readers to fail.

**D** is incorrect because VACUUM removes unreferenced data files and is a cleanup operation, not a write operation; it does not stage new data and does not participate in atomic data replacement.

[Upsert into a Delta Lake table using merge](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/merge)
questions.reference: [Upsert into a Delta Lake table using merge](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/merge)

questions.stem: A team is designing a fact table for a high-volume event stream. Select TWO design decisions that BEST balance query performance and write scalability on Delta Lake. (Select TWO)

options.text: Partition the table by event_date so partition pruning eliminates entire days for date-range queries
Partition the table by event_id (UUID) to maximize write parallelism
Apply liquid clustering on the most common filter columns rather than static date partitioning
Use OPTIMIZE with ZORDER BY the most selective filter columns to co-locate frequently co-queried values

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because partitioning a high-volume event table by a low-cardinality column like event_date (365 values per year) creates a manageable number of partitions while enabling very effective partition pruning for date-range queries, which are typical for event analytics.

**B** is incorrect because partitioning by UUID event_id creates as many partitions as there are events (potentially billions), which creates extreme small-file problems and listing overhead — this is a classic over-partitioning anti-pattern.

**C** is correct because liquid clustering adapts to evolving query patterns without requiring static partition boundaries; it co-locates rows with similar clustering key values across files, providing data skipping for multi-dimensional filters.

**D** is correct — actually, D is also a valid technique (ZORDER is an effective data skipping strategy) but the question asks for TWO from A, B, C, D. Between the four options, A and C are the BEST pair: A for date-based pruning which is standard practice, and C for flexible multi-dimensional skipping. D is valid but somewhat superseded by liquid clustering (C) on modern Databricks.

[Use liquid clustering for tables](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/clustering)

questions.stem: A senior engineer reviews a Databricks job that reads from three silver tables using Structured Streaming and joins them using stateful stream-stream joins with watermarks. The job has been running for 6 months and state store size is growing unboundedly. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: The streaming job is not configured with a checkpoint and accumulates state in driver memory
The watermark is not defined on all input streams in the join, preventing state from being evicted when events age out
OPTIMIZE is not running on the silver tables, so file accumulation is being counted as state store growth
Delta Sharing is enabled on the silver tables, causing the join state to be replicated to external recipients

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the job has been running for 6 months, implying checkpoints are working (the job would have failed on restart without one); the state store is on disk, not purely in driver memory.

**B** is correct because for stream-stream joins to evict state, Structured Streaming requires a watermark on both (all) input streams; if any stream in the join lacks a watermark, the engine cannot determine when it is safe to evict old join-candidate rows, causing the state store to grow without bound as events accumulate.

**C** is incorrect because OPTIMIZE compacts Delta data files on disk; the Structured Streaming state store is a separate RocksDB-backed store and its size is entirely independent of the Delta table file count.

**D** is incorrect because Delta Sharing is an outbound data sharing protocol; it does not interact with or replicate internal Spark streaming state stores.

[Structured Streaming checkpoints](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/structured-streaming/checkpoints)
